title: Spaghetti Aglio, Olio e Peperoncino
subtitle: The Roman midnight classic — garlic, chilli, and good oil, nothing more
description: Spaghetti aglio, olio e peperoncino is the purest demonstration of Italian cooking logic: great olive oil, patience over a gentle heat, and pasta water as a building material. Thinly sliced garlic is coaxed slowly in oil until golden and fragrant, dried chilli added at the end, then a ladleful of starchy pasta water emulsifies everything into a glossy, coating sauce. No cheese, no cream — the technique is the recipe.
sourceNotes: A staple of Roman and Southern Italian cucina povera. The dish is referenced in Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina (1891) and remains one of the canonical examples of the Italian cucina povera tradition. Technique is consistent across all credible Italian cookery sources.

recipeTools.notes: For boiling the pasta. Needs to hold at least 3 litres of water.
Wide enough to finish the pasta in and toss it. 28–30 cm works well.
For slicing the garlic as thinly as possible.
For transferring the spaghetti directly from the pot to the frying pan.

glossaryTerms.definition: The starchy, salted water pasta cooks in. Added to a sauce, it thickens and emulsifies the fat and liquid into a coating consistency.
Italian for garlic. In this dish the garlic is sliced rather than crushed or minced — thin slices go golden without burning, and remain visible in the finished dish.

ingredients.notes: For the pasta water — it should taste of the sea

steps: Bring a large pot of water to the boil and salt it generously.
Add the spaghetti and cook until 2 minutes short of the packet time — it will finish in the pan.

While the pasta cooks, set a large frying pan over the lowest possible heat. Add the olive oil and the sliced garlic. The garlic should sizzle very gently — not aggressively fry. Cook for 6–8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until golden and fragrant. If it colours too fast, remove the pan from the heat.
Add the chilli flakes for the final 30 seconds of the garlic's cooking time.
Scoop out a full ladle (about 120 ml) of pasta water and reserve it. Transfer the spaghetti directly to the frying pan with tongs — some water clinging to the pasta is welcome.
Increase the heat to medium and toss the pasta continuously, adding pasta water a splash at a time, until the sauce is glossy and coats each strand. This should take 1–2 minutes.
Remove from the heat. Add the parsley, toss once more, and serve immediately in warm bowls.

content.text: This is a recipe where heat management is everything. The garlic goes into cold oil and the heat is kept very low — the goal is golden and tender, never brown and bitter. Use the best extra-virgin olive oil you own; it is the sauce.
Bring a large pot of water to the boil. Salt it generously — the water should taste noticeably salty. Cook {{spaghetti}} of spaghetti until 2 minutes short of the packet time.
Set a large frying pan over the lowest heat. Pour in {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of extra-virgin olive oil and add the thinly sliced

. The oil should shimmer and the garlic sizzle gently. Cook for 6–8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the slices are pale gold and the oil smells deeply of garlic. If the garlic is colouring faster than this, lift the pan off the heat for 30 seconds. Add the dried chilli flakes for the final 30 seconds.
Scoop out a full ladle of

and set it beside the pan. Lift the spaghetti directly from the pot with tongs and drop it into the frying pan — do not shake off all the water. Increase the heat to medium and toss continuously, adding splashes of the reserved pasta water until the sauce is glossy and each strand is coated. This takes 1–2 minutes.
Remove from the heat, scatter over the chopped parsley, toss once more, and serve immediately in warm bowls.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: The two common failure modes are both about heat.

attrs.solution: The heat was too high. Sliced garlic burns in seconds once it passes golden. Start again — there is no rescuing burnt garlic in a dish with so few ingredients. Next time keep the flame as low as it goes and watch constantly.

attrs.problem: Sauce is greasy rather than glossy
attrs.solution: Not enough pasta water was used, or it was added too slowly. The starch in the water is what binds the oil into an emulsion. Add more pasta water and toss vigorously over a medium heat to bring the sauce together.
